chunk_summary: This section discusses medications and conditions that contraindicate breastfeeding, temporary interruptions, and management strategies to protect maternal milk supply and infant safety.
learning_objectives: Identify medications and conditions that contraindicate breastfeeding, including cytotoxic chemotherapy and radioisotopes.
Understand safe practices for temporary breastfeeding interruptions and milk expression.
Learn about the compatibility of anesthetic agents and breastfeeding post-procedure.
key_facts: Cytotoxic chemotherapy is a true contraindication to breastfeeding due to infant bone marrow suppression risks.
Radioisotopes and therapeutic radioiodine require breastfeeding interruption; duration depends on the isotope and nuclear medicine guidance.
Medications like amiodarone, lithium, ergot alkaloids, and high-dose sedatives/antipsychotics warrant avoidance or specialist consultation.
Breastfeeding is contraindicated with active cocaine or amphetamine use and unsafe with heavy alcohol intoxication.
Most anesthetic agents used for short procedures are compatible with breastfeeding once the mother is awake and stable.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: True contraindications to breastfeeding are uncommon and are usually time-limited. The highest-risk category is maternal cytotoxic chemotherapy because many agents are excreted in milk and can cause infant bone marrow suppression. (ABM Clinical Protocol #15)
